# Intro

# Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience: CX Agent Studio Foundations

[Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience: CX Agent Studio Foundations | Google Skills for Partners](https://partner.skills.google/paths/522) 

# **Course introduction**

## **Contact Centers of the Past**

Welcome to the course **Introduction to Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience**.

This course explores the different products and capabilities of Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience, including CX Agent Studio, Agent Assist and CX Insights. Additionally, it covers the foundational principles of conversation design to craft engaging and effective experiences that emulate human-like experiences specific to the Chat channel.

This course is designed for **developers** and **engineers** who have experience with Google Cloud. Prior knowledge of generative AI or Customer Experience (CX) Agents is helpful, but not required.  
In this course you will learn how to:  
**✓ **  Implement solutions using one or more of the Gemini Enterprise for CX products, including CX Agent Studio, Agent Assist and CX Insights.

**✓ **  Design conversations for chat-based agents.

**✓**   Discuss the benefits of using Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience instead of traditional Contact Center solutions.

00:00

Let’s take a look at the ways in which contact centers have evolved over time.

00:07

In this module, you will explore legacy contact centers and the common challenges they present.

00:12

You will then learn how Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience provides targeted solutions for these outdated systems.

00:20

Finally, you will step into the future to discover how Generative AI is fundamentally transforming contact center operations today.

00:30

Let’s get started\!

00:33

Customer service is a pressured market, looking for a new paradigm to overcome structural challenges.

00:39

In most cases, contact centers are also viewed as a cost center.

00:44

Any effort devoted to digital transformation has struggled to keep pace with the rapid innovation and

00:50

demand for customer support services which has seen a steep increase over the last 5-10 years.

00:58

The customer experience is often handled by legacy systems stitched with incremental technologies that are not always optimally interoperable.

01:09

As a result of this, customers are often faced with challenges that negatively impact their experience.

01:15

Let’s take some time to understand these customer challenges.

01:20

The first challenge that customers face when dealing with legacy contact centers systems is the lack of a unified experience.

01:28

Customers nowadays expect to connect with companies throughout multiple online channels and with a consistent experience.

01:38

Second is rigid decision trees.

01:40

Traditional contact center technology suffers from a lack of flexibility from the predesigned paths.

01:46

It provides experiences that are far from human like and end up creating frustration for the user.

01:55

Another obstacle is increased expectations.

01:57

Users prefer the convenience and speed of on-demand services and answers to their questions.

02:06

And lastly, there’s the issue of dropped, repeat and unresolved calls.

02:10

These lead to customers facing the frustration of repeatedly explaining the same issue to multiple support agents.

02:18

By observing these challenges, it’s easy to derive that the customer-centric contact center was a distant dream for many organizations.

02:29

Next, let’s focus on the infrastructure challenges that underline all this complexity.

02:36

The most common of these infrastructure challenges include legacy systems that are often neglected, rarely upgraded, and difficult to integrate.

02:46

Additionally, there is a business logic that sits on top of these systems which is convoluted and challenging to maintain.

02:56

Thirdly, stringent infrastructure and security protocols often add friction to transformation, given the fact that contact centers house a large amount of personal identifiable data, or PII.

03:11

Lastly, the poor interoperability between the patchwork of systems often stitched together makes it difficult to retrieve and analyze data effectively.

03:20

Retrieving a simple piece of information can take valuable minutes or hours, frustrating agents and customers alike.

03:28

These invisible walls legacy systems, tangled business logic, weak security, and data fragmentation are the silent enemies of efficiency, agility, and customer experience.

03:43

Human agent challenges are often overlooked, but they play a critical role in the current status quo of many contact centers.

03:53

Contact center agents often face a number of challenges that frustrate their employee experience and are at the root of the high attrition rate in contact centers.

04:03

The first of these challenges is Productivity.

04:06

Agents have to manage multiple slow systems, and bounce between browser tabs to accurately manage customer calls.

04:15

There’s also the issue of limited flexibility, where agents are limited in what they can say and do, and are almost always measured on this.

04:26

The third issue involves accurate context.

04:27

The majority of agents attend to calls without sufficient information about the customer’s case.

04:34

As a result of this, agents often have to start from the beginning to collect the customer’s information.

04:42

And a final challenge is data collection and documentation, where agents often have to update multiple systems and summarize their calls in a timely manner.

04:51

Agents are the heart of a contact center, but their tools often limit their ability to efficiently handle customer cases.

05:00

By providing accurate context and streamlined data collection, they’ll have the ability to transform their experiences, turning them into empowered customer champions.

05:13

Lastly, it is important to mention AI challenges.

05:19

AI as a concept and technology is not new in the contact center, but few have been able to harness its full potential in the right way.

05:27

The Chatbot revolution started around 2016, when many businesses started to heavily invest in chatbots.

05:37

The first challenge that they encountered were the underwhelming results, following overhyped promises on performance.

05:44

This is often referred to as “Chatbot hangover”.

05:49

Second was maturity.

05:51

Given the novelty of the technology, many businesses were missing the required expertise on what it takes to build a highly catered conversational experience.

06:02

The third challenge was the lack of availability or engagement of the right set of resources.

06:08

Building bots was not a trivial task, and required a deep expertise in machine learning, linguistics and data science.

06:16

However, leaders still cite focusing on customer service as a top priority.

06:23

Given all the challenges that Contact centers faced in the past, and the critical role that Contact Centers play in

06:29

the future of enterprise; focusing on customer service by improving the customer experience is a key priority for many businesses today.

06:39

Large investments are being deployed to craft customer experiences that meet the high-quality standards that customers expect nowadays.

06:49

This renewed focus on customer service is expected to bring consistent operational improvement and top line revenue growth for enterprises.

06:59

According to a study from Gartner, 63% of service and support leaders put the improvement of current self-service experiences as front and center in their operational strategies.

07:09

58% already have plans in place to pivot their customer service function to improve its contribution to top-line revenue growth through new digital transformation.

07:22

Google Cloud's Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience, can help transform an enterprise’s customer service by offering the following solutions: First, it provides an interaction experience that is close to human.

07:36

This is far from the robotic, pre-recorded voices that often support the customer service experience of many call centers.

07:47

Then there is adaptive routing to reduce the need for human escalation, as compared to the previous rules-based approach.

07:56

Third, it has the ability to empower human agents with the right information to resolve customer queries as fast and efficiently as possible.

08:06

It also consists of auto-tuned, AI driven speech models that do not require regular tuning.

08:15

Finally, Google Cloud can also ensure an omni channel experience through the integration to the billing and operations systems in the same platform.

08:26

To summarize, investing in a new, AI powered customer service experience can potentially deliver 4 key benefits: The first is to boost agent and employee productivity.

08:38

The second is to improve self-service and deflection rates.

08:42

The third benefit is to modernize chat and voice infrastructure.

08:46

And finally, the fourth benefit is to enhance insights and customer predictions.

08:51

Good conversational experiences lead to better customer experiences which in turn greatly enhance the ability of enterprises to thrive both financially and operationally.

00:00

## **Contact Centers of the Future**

So, how can Google Cloud enterprises “enter the future”?

00:05

A new era has begun with “Industrial Revolution 4-point-0” driven by Artificial Intelligence.

00:12

This revolution is considered as impactful as the three preceding it, starting from the steam power revolution

00:18

in 1784, followed by the electricity revolution in 1870 and the first information technology revolution in 1969\.

00:29

This new era is defined by intuitive human-to-computer interactions powered by a new field of Artificial Intelligence called Generative AI.

00:39

Generative AI is capable of understanding and generating language through modalities such as text, audio, video and images.

00:49

As AI transforms contact centers, it is necessary to have a framework that breaks down user self-service functionalities into 3 categories: Informational, Transactional, and Generative.

01:05

Informational functionalities serve use cases where the user wants to know something.

01:11

Conversely, transactional functionalities serve use cases where the user wants to do something.

01:18

While, generative functionalities serve use cases both informational and transactional.

01:27

Keep in mind that these are not mutually exclusive.

01:30

One experience can be informational, transactional, and generative.

01:34

Essentially, all three work together to transform the contact center experience.

01:42

For instance, a user calls in to check their bill balance (informational), then asks to pay their bill (transactional), with generative capabilities used for conversational fallout.

01:54

To the user this is one conversation, but in order to develop and deploy to production it needs to be broken down into these categories.

02:03

From here, you can implement generative technologies into both informational transactional use cases to create new wonderful experiences.

02:14

Behind the scenes, the platform that powers all the Generative AI functionalities in Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience is Vertex AI, which is Google Cloud’s AI platform.

02:27

This is a collection of APIs and tools that supports all the needs of the new Generative AI-centric enterprise.

02:34

Starting at the bottom of the stack, we have Google’s world class AI optimized infrastructure, fully integrated with Google Cloud infrastructure.

02:45

On top of it sits the Vertex AI Model Garden, a one stop shop to find first-party, open source and third-party large language models.

02:55

The Vertex AI Platform leverages the resources from the Model Garden to provide end-to-end tooling to build, deploy and monitor Gen AI models and pipelines.

03:07

Agent Builder is the interface that developers can then lean on to access the AI

03:12

platform resources to quickly and easily infuse chat, Gen AI, and search into their Enterprise applications.

03:22

At the top, we see AI solutions such as Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience where you can leverage CX Agent Studio to build chatbots and voicebots at scale.

03:33

These capabilities are both accessible as out-of-the-box solutions and custom agents.

3\.

The conversational core enables three AI powered products.

Agent Assist, Vertex AI, Insights

Insights, Vertex AI, Conversational Core

Insights, Conversational Agents, Conversational Core

4\.

Chatbot Hangover is \[ \]

When business initially built chatbots and had underwhelming results, following overhyped promises on performance.

Long standing and often neglected, rarely upgraded, and difficult to integrate systems.

Multiple (slow) systems and tabs to bounce between.

The lack of availability or engagement of the right set of resources.

5\.

A good Retail use case would be:

Customer wants to create a chatbot to answer Product FAQs.

A virtual nurse to help doctors with summarizations of medical records.

Customer wants to create an internal tool for searching and retrieving insurance codes based on patient symptoms.

Customers want an omni-channel solution to categorize and summarize their calls.

All questions must be answered before submitting.

00:00

## **Gemini Enterprise for CX Building Blocks**

Let’s dive into the Gemini Enterprise for Customer Experience building blocks CX agents operate within a unified Gemini Enterprise for CX API interface.

00:14

Anyone implementing CX Agent Studio can access the Gemini Enterprise for CX suite of products through

00:19

the Visual Builder Console and SDKs in various languages, such as Python, Go, Node, and many more.

00:28

The system is a Fully Managed Service powered by the Agent Development Kit (ADK) and the latest Gemini models.

00:36

The key Customer Experience components include CX Agent Studio, Agent Assist, and Customer Experience Insights (CX Insights).

00:46

The architecture integrates through Contact Center as a Service (CCaaS) and features a layer for conversational data and common storage options.

00:57

Gemini Enterprise for CX is more than just building chatbots.

01:02

If you are looking to connect customers with AI agents or human agents, or if

01:06

you want to modernize your contact center, that’s when you should consider Gemini Enterprise for CX.

01:12

Gemini Enterprise for CX has 3 main components, AI agents via CX Agent Studio, AI tools for human agents via Agent Assist, and historical charts and analytics with CX Insights.

01:30

Maybe you’re looking for help with scaling up your ability for self-serve in your call center and improving customer satisfaction without increasing your staffing levels?

01:40

If so, consider implementing a CX agent.

01:45

The next question to ask is “Are you looking for assistive technologies that enable your staff to do an efficient and better job at resolving your customers' needs?”

01:55

If so, consider using our Agent Assist capabilities.

02:00

Are you interested in performing deep analytics on how your customers are interacting with your Contact Center?

02:05

If so, consider using CX Insights to derive customer, agent, and contact center Key Performance Indicators, or KPIs.

02:18

If you are looking to manage all customer interactions, then Gemini Enterprise for CX together with Google Cloud CCaaS is what you are looking for.

02:27

While Gemini Enterprise for CX provides the most advanced AI features to automate customer interactions and help human agents

02:34

and managers do their work better, Google Cloud CCaaS is the tool that human agents will directly work with.

02:43

Google Cloud CCaaS is a ticket management system for human agents to interact with customers.

02:50

Human agents have a multimodal, omnichannel platform where they can connect and interact with customers and receive inbound and outbound voice, SMS, and chat messages.

03:01

Managers have access to a workforce and quality management solution to be on top of human agents performance.

03:10

The first pillar is CX Agent Studio.

03:12

CX Agent Studio provides a next-generation platform powered by Gemini to rapidly build, evaluate, and deploy highly personalized CX agents in days versus weeks.

03:25

It provides: A comprehensive development platform that uses AI to build multimodal AI agents in a visual interface.

03:34

Always on customer self-service from personalized agents with human-like voices in over 40 languages.

03:40

Retains context and insights from across the customer journey to enable proactive support.

03:47

Resolve customer inquiries with zero wait times and process information from text, audio, and images.

03:57

CX Agent Studio offers three benefits.

04:00

First, it delivers an on brand, omni-channel customer self-service experience for voice and chat by

04:06

getting answers to questions or completing transactions conversationally, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

04:16

Speeds development with prebuilt agent templates and AI that augments the entire agent development process.

04:25

And finally, scale self-service across regions with agents that understand intent and can converse in multiple languages.

04:35

The second pillar of the conversational core is Agent Assist, also known as the Empowering Coach.

04:41

With Agent Assist, human agents are actively supported during live conversations.

04:46

It analyzes customer intent and provides real-time suggestions and relevant information to the agent, aiding in delivering more efficient and effective customer interactions.

04:57

The NLU engine and the LLMs also fuel Agent Assist, adding an extra layer of intelligence.

05:08

In scenarios where live agent support remains necessary, Agent Assist facilitates a seamless transition from virtual to human interaction.

05:17

When a call cannot be resolved by the CX Agent, it is routed to a human agent.

05:22

This continuous support approach empowers human agents with transcripts of the customer-CX Agent conversation, allowing them to continue the interaction without repeating questions.

05:35

Additionally, Agent Assist provides real-time assistance to human agents.

05:40

This includes delivering relevant information, guiding workflows, and offering turn-by-turn suggestions.

05:48

This ensures a consistent customer experience.

05:53

Lastly, by rating these smart replies for usefulness, live agents contribute to the model's learning, improving the accuracy of assistance over time.

06:04

The final pillar is CX Insights: The Knowledge Vault.

06:08

Gemini Enterprise for CX Insights acts as a powerful analytics engine for Contact Centers.

06:14

It processes and analyzes historical conversation data, providing you with actionable insights.

06:21

This enables you to identify optimization opportunities, improve operational efficiency, and enhance the overall customer experience.

06:32

CX Insights can greatly empower a business by providing a live dashboard with stats like the number of calls received, the percentage split among intents, key compliance phrases and more.

06:44

These metrics, enabled by Natural Language Processing, help businesses understand their customers better.

06:53

CX Insights also assists businesses with various compliance requirements by identifying key phrases to help operations and QA stay on top of the enterprise’s most important obligations.

07:07

Lastly, Insights provides intelligence to businesses by surfacing sentiment and language data on how live agents interact with customers.

07:16

So, there you have it, Google Cloud’s Gemini Enterprise for CX, a three-pillar powerhouse designed to elevate your Contact Center to new heights.

07:27

Remember, it's not just about replacing humans; it's about empowering them with AI to create a seamless, efficient, and ultimately, customer-centric journey.

07:39

Now, let’s review some use cases that these technologies can help unlock.

07:46

In this example, a clinic wants to implement a conversational interface to allow clinicians seamless access to patient information.

07:53

Examples of possible use cases include: A virtual nurse to help doctors with summarizations of medical records.

08:02

An AI assistant to review patient scans, x-rays, and charts to help determine best treatment options.

08:10

And a business that wants to create an internal tool for searching and retrieving insurance codes based on patient symptoms.

08:18

In this example, a clinic wants to implement a conversational interface to allow clinicians seamless access to patient information.

08:21

Examples of possible use cases include: A virtual nurse to help doctors with summarizations of medical records.

08:28

An AI assistant to review patient scans, x-rays, and charts to help determine best treatment options.

08:38

And a business that wants to create an internal tool for searching and retrieving insurance codes based on patient symptoms.

08:42

Retail is another industry primed for disruption with AI in customer services.

08:47

In this example, a fast food chain wants to streamline drive-thru ordering for a frictionless customer experience.

08:53

Examples of use cases include: A business that wants to create a chatbot to answer Product FAQs.

09:01

A business such as a large restaurant chain that wants to create a multi-modal food order taking experience.

09:08

A business is unsure why customers are calling in, and wants to identify conversational topics and areas to focus self-service efforts.

09:18

Lastly, AI powered CX Agents are poised to transform customer service in the Telecommunications industry.

09:25

Some telco CX Agents use case examples are: A business looking to build a voice virtual

09:31

assistant for their contact center operations spanning across multiple domains like Billing, Payments, Troubleshooting, and Accounts.

09:40

A business that wants real-time suggestions for call center agents to decrease average handle time.

09:45

And a business that requires an omni-channel solution to categorize and summarize their calls.

09:53

These use cases provide clear benefits to both the enterprise and the customers.

00:00

## **Go-To-Market Strategy**

Now that you have a foundational knowledge of key products and features of the Gemini Enterprise for CX solution, let’s delve into its go-to-market strategy.

00:12

In this lesson, you’ll explore the Customer Service Modernization Solution, the new Google Cloud Consulting Go-To-Market strategy.

00:20

This comprises of a number of service packages that leverage specific Gemini Enterprise for CX and Google Cloud

00:27

products to create value for customer service use cases, which you’ll also get to explore throughout this module.

00:36

As part of Google Cloud’s evolving Go-To-Market, Gemini Enterprise for CX has become a central set of products which combine to create the Customer Service Modernization AI Solution.

00:48

Sellers across the globe have learned about the value that Google Cloud can unlock when Google Cloud Consulting and Partners leverage Gemini Enterprise for CX to modernize the customer experience.

01:01

The Customer Service Modernization Solution helps customers to improve self service and deflection rates.

01:09

Customers can easily access enterprise and product data to boost agent productivity and improve customer response time, as well

01:17

as access a digital concierge capable of delivering a superior, human-like customer experience across both chat and voice channels.

01:28

It can also be used to modernize chat and voice infrastructure.

01:32

Customers can quickly replace their legacy interactive voice response systems, modernizing the overall chat and voice infrastructure.

01:42

Additionally, it helps to boost agent productivity.

01:45

Frontline customer service agents’ productivity can be greatly enhanced by providing real-time assistance through quick access to enterprise data.

01:56

And finally, it also helps by providing real-time customer service insights.

00:00

## **Greenfield and Brownfield Customers**

Next, you’ll unpack what greenfield and brownfield implementations look like with Gemini Enterprise for CX.

00:10

In this lesson, you’ll learn to: Define greenfield & brownfield customers.

00:16

Understand greenfield and brownfield use case examples and their advantages & disadvantages.

00:21

And identify a greenfield or brownfield customer.

00:25

Let’s get started.

00:29

Greenfield customers are those who are building their customer contact center strategy from scratch.

00:34

They typically have no legacy systems or infrastructure in place and they are free to choose the best technology solutions for their needs.

00:45

Conversely, Brownfield customers are those who are already operating customer contact centers that have existing systems and infrastructure in place.

00:55

They may be looking to upgrade their systems or add new capabilities, but they need to do so in a way that is compatible with their existing environment.

01:03

For both greenfield and brownfield customers, Google Gemini Enterprise for CX can offer a number of benefits.

01:14

Some example Greenfield use cases are: A startup company that is building its first chatbot.

01:20

A consumer goods company that is expanding into a new market and has no current CX Agent architecture.

01:26

And a company that is already a Google Cloud customer but wants to leverage Gemini Enterprise for CX tools for internal FAQs.

01:38

There are many advantages to being a greenfield customer.

01:42

To start with, greenfield customers have a clean slate.

01:46

As a greenfield customer, you’ll start with a blank canvas with minimal restrictions from existing systems or processes.

01:54

This freedom allows for innovative solutions, and the ability to implement best practices without being constrained by the past.

02:02

Additionally, greenfield environments foster agility, allowing them to move and iterate quickly due to the lack of legacy systems.

02:11

This enables rapid experimentation and adaptation to changing requirements.

02:16

In some cases, greenfield customers present larger opportunities.

02:21

Without existing customer relationships or sales history, there is potential for significant growth and expansion.

02:30

However, there are also disadvantages to being a greenfield customer.

02:34

Defining the scope of a greenfield project can be a challenge.

02:38

Without established parameters, it may be difficult to set clear goals and measure success.

02:44

Greenfield customers often face data readiness issues as there may not be sufficient historical data to support decision-making.

02:52

Furthermore, finding skilled resources can be difficult in greenfield environments.

02:57

You may need people with specific technical expertise or domain knowledge that is not yet widely available.

03:05

Next, let’s analyze the same information for brownfield customers.

03:10

Some brownfield example use cases are: A Fortune 500 company that has an existing conversational agent structure already integrated into legacy systems.

03:22

A consumer goods company that wants to upgrade its customer Contact Center with new GenAI features and capabilities.

03:31

And a large healthcare company that currently has a multi-channel experience outside of Google Cloud products.

03:39

Some of the advantages for brownfield customers include the following.

03:44

Brownfield customers benefit from existing infrastructure.

03:48

You can leverage established systems to implement new features and capabilities, which saves time and resources compared to building from scratch.

03:57

Brownfield environments also offer the opportunity to work with real-world data.

04:02

You can experiment and iterate rapidly, using production data to validate solutions and drive improvements.

04:09

Typically, brownfield customers have a deep understanding of their business.

04:14

This existing knowledge base can be invaluable in defining requirements and designing solutions that truly meet their needs.

04:23

There are also disadvantages with brownfield customers.

04:27

Integrating with legacy systems can be a major challenge.

04:30

Outdated or incompatible systems may create complexities and slow down development.

04:36

In brownfield environments, you often encounter complex business logic built up over time.

04:43

This can make it difficult to understand and modify systems without introducing unintended consequences.

04:47

The risk of disruption can be higher when deploying changes in a brownfield environment.

04:55

There is a greater likelihood of impacting existing systems or processes, requiring careful planning and testing.

05:04

Part of the challenge when trying to identify greenfield versus brownfield use cases is related

05:09

to the fact that conversational experiences never just leverage one platform or solution like CX Agents.

05:18

CX Agents rarely work alone without calling Apigee or Pub/Sub or other Google Cloud products.

05:25

As illustrated in this example diagram, when a user chats with a CX Agent Studio chatbot, there

05:31

are many systems and tools that interact with the front-end and back-end systems to create a seamless experience.

05:38

The “customer-client”, “chat router”, and “back-end CRM” could be different types of systems, but ultimately

05:45

they are connecting the user with the enterprise data and CX Agents to create a conversation.

05:52

In this example, the chat application is attached through multiple layers of “UI” engines and platforms, integrating different cloud solutions.

06:01

So you must understand all of the components to the delivery.

06:07

To summarize, determining whether a client is considered greenfield or brownfield requires a deep understanding of the client infrastructure and depends on the use case.

06:18

Most enterprises will have both greenfield and brownfield use cases.

00:00

## **The Business Case**

Next, let’s zoom in on the business case around Gemini Enterprise for CX.

00:07

When defining the business case for a contact center, it’s good to look at customer service priorities through 4 lenses and to consider some key questions.

00:16

The first is Scale.

00:19

When analyzing scale, you could ask the following questions: Are interaction volume and average handle time primary concerns?

00:27

And are you looking to improve agent C-SAT (shorthand for customer satisfaction) or occupancy rate?

00:37

Next is cost.

00:38

Are costs per customer interaction, back office expenses, or overall customer volume interaction costs driving decision-making?

00:49

Also of importance is experience.

00:50

Is improving your customer satisfaction rates, reducing customer churn, and optimizing interaction channels your primary concern?

01:02

Finally, there’s Growth.

01:03

Consider the following question: Are increasing revenue with upsell and cross-opportunities as well as decreasing your customer acquisition costs important?

01:15

Once the priorities are identified, there are many metrics used in the contact center space to measure their degree of achievement.

01:22

Ideally the goal of any use case is to decrease cost while increasing efficiency, revenue and overall customer experience.

01:32

So of these numerous metrics, the ones that are most commonly monitored are: volumes, average handle time, and containment rate.

01:41

Let’s dive deeper into each of these.

01:45

Volume is the number of contacts coming into a specific channel (either chat or voice) for a specific interaction, function, intent or agent group.

01:57

Average handle time is how long it takes a live agent to complete a customer interaction.

02:04

Containment can be defined as the percentage of users that are able to successfully complete a self-service function without the need for human intervention.

02:13

Most contact centers will have immediate containment numbers.

02:20

This is not always 100% accurate for determining if a user was successful because users can and do call back when their issue is not resolved.

02:29

Because of this, most contact centers evaluate 48 hours or 72 hours containment for business priorities.

02:38

48 hours or 72 hours containment is a better indicator of a user success because it tracks

02:45

if the user has contacted the company more than once for the same intent within a certain timeframe.

02:53

Most enterprises will have some layer of business logic that filters out certain types of users.

03:00

So when speaking about volumes, it is crucial that you evaluate not just Total volume but also analyze addressable volume.

03:07

Addressable volume is the total of contacts after screening out for various business rules.

03:13

The enterprise may also already have an incumbent solution containing calls (also known as baseline).

03:20

This would also subtract volumes for the new solution.

03:24

So in order to have a clear understanding of the potential impact of your new solution, you must look at Total

03:30

Volume, Addressable Volume, and Baseline Contained to derive a realistic estimate of the total number of calls going to self service.

03:40

Total Volume is the top of the funnel traffic per function.

03:43

Addressable Volume is after business logic screening.

03:46

Baseline Contained are the calls already going to self-service.

03:51

And Potential impact is the final volume number.

03:56

Average handle time is another key metric used to evaluate the performance of a contact center.

04:02

It measures how long it takes a live agent to complete a customer interaction.

04:08

It is generally measured as a percentage or total time in minutes, and calculated as the

04:12

cumulative total time spent on calls or interactions divided by the number of calls or interactions.

04:21

The longer Average Handle Time the more complex the interaction.

04:26

Lastly, Containment can be defined as the percentage of customers that are able to successfully complete a self-service function without the need for human intervention.

04:39

Let’s analyze a quick example of containment.

04:42

Sarah asks to move her billing date from the fifth to the twelfth.

04:47

The CX Agent intelligently processes Sarah’s request by updating her billing date and Ads profile.

04:56

With the Billing date moved, Sarah concludes the call.

04:59

She never interacted with a human agent, so Sarah’s contacts are contained.

05:07

The reason why containment is often deemed to be the most critical metric in a business leveraging Gemini

05:12

Enterprise for CX, is because its benefits touch every point in the customer and the human agent journey.

05:20

Containing more calls allows agents to focus on the most complex use cases.

05:27

This includes the ones with potential for up and cross selling which increases the average revenue per user or ARPU.

05:37

A higher containment usually indicates a good customer experience with low churn and abandonment.

05:45

Where customers are happy, agents are happy, and their rate of attrition tends to decrease by an estimated 5 to 7%.

05:53

It’s a win-win situation for everyone, which is why Gemini Enterprise for CX is often positioned as the end-to-end solution for all contact centers’ needs.

06:05

A tactical way to determine the opportunity for Gemini Enterprise for CX is to create a

06:11

small business case leveraging the Contact center call volume, average handle time, and average hourly rate.

06:18

With these three numbers you can create a simple equation to determine the dollar value of the opportunity.

06:24

For example, a customer gets 1000 contacts a day about their product FAQs.

06:32

The average handle time is 3 minutes and the live agents cost an average of 21 dollars and 95 cents an hour.

06:40

This is over 1000 dollars of cost per day on answering product FAQs.

06:48

Now let’s imagine that this company also provides custom designs and manufacturing for flooring components.

06:54

In this case they have 100 contacts per day, each call takes 45 minutes and the average live agent cost is 21 dollars and 95 cents per hour.

07:07

The net cost is over 1600 dollars per day in opportunity.

07:11

So the custom jobs customer service is on average more expensive than the Product FAQ call center experience, despite having one-tenth of the incoming volumes.

07:22

This indicates that the volume alone is not a great indicator of opportunity size.

07:28

From this example, it would be more valuable to focus on custom jobs than product FAQs.

00:00

## **Use Cases and User Personas**

When assessing the opportunity to embrace Gemini Enterprise for CX as a solution for a business,

00:06

it is important to think about use cases and user personas to best target development efforts.

00:13

Let’s analyze the details of these use cases and user personas.

00:19

In this lesson, you’ll learn to define a use case before analyzing how to best leverage design thinking to create user-centered solutions.

00:28

Finally, you’ll learn how to prioritize work using the delta use case canvas approach.

00:36

First, what is a use case?

00:38

A use case is a description of how technology can be leveraged to address a specific user need.

00:45

Use cases typically include the following information: A description of the goal that the user is trying to achieve.

00:54

A list of the steps that the user takes to achieve the goal.

00:58

A description of the system's responses to the user's actions.

01:02

Any preconditions or assumptions that must be met in order for the use case to be successful.

01:08

And any postconditions that must be met after the use case has been completed.

01:14

A user persona is a fictional character that represents a typical user of a product or service underpinning a use case.

01:24

Personas are generally based on real-world research and are used to help businesses understand their target

01:29

audience in order to create products and services that are more likely to meet their needs.

01:35

Displaying empathy for a user is a great way to create products and services that are successful in the market as more user-friendly and enjoyable to use.

01:46

Personas can help you to make better decisions about your products and services.

01:50

For example, you can use personas to test new features or decide how to best cater a marketing message.

01:59

To properly leverage your user persona when designing conversational applications, you need to consider the following points.

02:07

Understand the brand by interviewing every possible stakeholder.

02:10

Different stakeholders will have different perspectives on the brand.

02:14

Then review the marketing material, the company website, commercials, and anything else open to the public.

02:21

If possible, visit the contact center as well.

02:27

Then understand the customer by asking the following questions.

02:29

First, what are their needs?

02:33

Then, what does their customer journey look like?

02:35

And also, what is their demographic and degree of engagement?

02:41

Once you understand the customer you can start to understand the task or the intent of the user.

02:46

In other words, what are users mostly reaching out to the contact center about?

02:54

Finally, create characters that represent the users and stakeholders across the contact centers engagement journey.

03:00

For example, Contact Center Managers, Call Center Agents, Gen X Customers and Legacy Customers.

03:08

All this information can be used to build a persona for your CX Agent that best caters to your “real” user personas.

03:18

In building a CX Agent persona, the questions you want to answer are as follows.

03:22

What’s the name of your CX Agent, and is there any meaning behind this name?

03:30

What’s the age or age range of this CX Agent?

03:33

This informs the agent’s tone (like energetic or sophisticated.) What’s the gender of your CX Agent?

03:42

Knowing the gender of the CX Agent helps create the custom voice.

03:48

Does the service that your CX Agent provides require any special training?

03:53

What role will this CX Agent play in your organization, (like general customer representative or technical support?) Note that the goal

04:01

of creating a profile for the CX Agent is not to trick the user into thinking they’re talking to a human being.

04:09

The profile is there to serve as a guide to help conversation designers write dialog with a uniform communication style.

04:19

Here is an example user persona for Betty, an early-to-mid-thirties female.

04:23

She is approachable, happy and friendly.

04:26

From her profile, you can see that she is also trustworthy, reliable and consistent.

04:31

Furthermore, she provides convenience and low effort customer service, and is also patient without rushing the customer.

04:40

Her interactions are simple, and straightforward.

04:43

This role is best catered as a general customer service representative that can route customers to other service branches.

### Quiz

Passing score: 80%

1\.

One advantage of being a Greenfield customer is

Infrastructure exists so they can implement new features and capabilities without having to rip and replace their entire system.

Not constrained to legacy business or system requirements.

Can experiment and iterate quickly with production data.

Understands the business use case.

2\.

Modernizing chat and voice infrastructure includes…

Replace legacy Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems

Deploy Gen-AI powered Conversational Agents

Deploy Cloud and Data Foundations

All options are correct

3\.

The Top 3 Business Metrics are…

Call Volume, Total Handle Time, Containment

Scale, Average Handle Time, Containment

Addressable Volume, 24hr Containment, 48hr Containment

Call Volume, Average Handle Time, Containment

4\.

Enterprises are…

Brownfield opportunities only.

Both Brownfield and Greenfield opportunities.

Greenfield opportunities only.

Neither Brownfield or Greenfield opportunities.

5\.

One advantage of being a Brownfield customer is

Possible larger opportunities.

Can move and iterate quickly.

Have a complete clean slate with minimal restrictions.

Can experiment and iterate quickly with production data.

6\.

The four business priorities for customer service are…

Average Handle Time, Containment, Volume, Hourly Rate

Scale, Cost, Experience, Growth

Scale, Revenue, Containment, Growth

Cost, Experience, Growth, Hourly Rate

All questions must be answered before submitting.

00:00

## **Conversation Design Fundamentals**

Welcome to this course on Conversation Design Fundamentals.

00:04

Please note that Conversational agents are also referred to as virtual agents.

00:08

This training will guide you through the essential elements of crafting engaging and effective chat experiences that emulate human like experiences.

00:18

We'll start with a comprehensive introduction to conversation design or CXD, followed by strategies you can use to align your chat design with your business objectives.

00:28

You'll learn how to create user personas, write compelling chat scripts, and enhance each conversational turn.

00:35

Additionally, we'll cover the best ways to write guardrails that keep conversations on track and how to gracefully handle situations where the chat bot cannot fulfill an end user's request.

00:46

So much to cover, so let's jump right in\!

00:49

Let's start by exploring the role that Gen AI can play in human-like conversation design.

00:54

As mentioned, this deck is specific to chat conversational experiences, but the considerations and principles that follow are both applicable to chat and voice.

01:04

Let's review the key functionalities of Large Language Models or LLMs, and the role they play in configuring virtual agent interactions.

01:13

LLMs are typically generalists trained on a large corpus of text.

01:18

This allows users to prompt or chat with LLMs in a divergent way across a vast range of topics.

01:25

However, what if you're actually trying to solve a problem, like reporting a property damage?

01:31

What seems like creativity and open ended possibilities might turn into a frustrating user experience.

01:37

You can see this in the following example, where a user initiates a conversation to report a graffiti on the fence of his or her property.

01:46

The agent, powered by LLM, responds showing empathy for the situation, but it offers too much context and options that can disengage the user.

01:55

Like listing multiple file sharing sites.

01:58

On the other hand, if we were to only use deterministic intent based design, our chatbot might get the

02:03

job done, but users would feel like they were conversing with an impersonal robot rather than a helpful assistant.

02:11

This is an example of a conversation built through deterministic, intent based design.

02:16

It provides roughly the same information to the user as the prior example.

02:20

However, it sounds more impersonal and ends up with a no match at the end.

02:25

This is because it cannot handle a scenario in which the user doesn't know how to upload a photo to a sharing site.

02:32

These two examples highlight the difference between divergence and convergence in the context of communication.

02:39

They allow us to explore the difference between open ended conversations that help users learn, versus goal oriented conversations that help users solve a specific problem.

02:49

Convergent thinking focuses on reaching one well-defined solution to a problem.

02:54

This type of thinking is best suited for tasks that involve logic as opposed to creativity.

03:00

For example, solving a problem where you know there are no other possible solutions.

03:04

In this vignette, these two people are discussing going out for dinner.

03:09

Finally, they agree on cooking at home.

03:12

Divergent thinking is the opposite of convergent thinking and involves more creativity.

03:17

There are certainly advantages related to divergent thinking.

03:21

It can help you identify new opportunities and find creative ways to solve problems.

03:26

However, as we can see in the vignette, divergent thinking might take these two people far away from reaching a common goal.

03:33

When we're designing conversations with users, we want to ensure that we're divergent when it comes to

03:38

options and possibilities, and convergent when we're trying to help them solve a problem or make transactions.

03:45

If we apply these two concepts to LLMs and virtual agents, it is reasonable to believe that divergent thinking is

03:51

more suitable for creative, open ended conversations like those that help users learn or find answers to frequently asked questions.

04:00

While virtual agents in the more traditional sense, are suited for focused, goal oriented conversations that help users resolve a specific problem offered by convergent thinking.

04:11

Let's explore an example of a virtual agent that is designed to be convergent.

04:15

In this example, the user's goal is to book a liveaboard for his or her family.

04:20

Notice how the agent is not too prescriptive.

04:23

Instead, they ask the user where they would like to go.

04:27

Thanks to LLMs, the agent is able to handle an unexpected turn in which the user requests a destination the agent can't help with.

04:35

The agent effectively resets the expectation about what is and what isn't possible.

04:41

Steering the conversation back to the successful path.

04:45

It would be extremely hard and almost impossible to design an agent to handle the myriad of unexpected user inputs, such as this one.

04:53

That's where Gen AI comes into play.

04:56

Now let's review an example where the agent is designed to handle a more deterministic and goal oriented conversation.

05:03

The user wants to go snorkeling with their family in January.

05:07

Notice how the agent switches to a generative and LLM based approach when it's appropriate.

05:12

Once the question is answered or the distraction is over, the agent returns to helping the user with her primary goal.

05:22

In light of these considerations, when designing a virtual agent that puts the customer's experience first, it is important to always leverage Gen AI for the long tail.

05:33

This peacock's visual should depict this for you.

05:36

The head is represented by the key use cases that make 20% of the possible paths in a dialog flow.

05:43

These are the most important and most common conversational paths that 80% of users follow.

05:48

When designing a conversational interface, focus the majority of your effort on making these paths a great user experience.

05:56

These are your critical use cases.

05:58

For example, use cases that allow you to report a lost passport or request to block a stolen credit card.

06:06

Transactional use cases like these are centered around clear and specific intents.

06:11

These intents often require deterministic prompts to collect and validate structured data needed to trigger actions on the back end.

06:20

The inability to detect the user's intent or extract the contextual data would inevitably build frustration with the user, so it's

06:28

probably not a good idea to delegate the whole user agent interaction of these use cases to an open ended LLM.

06:36

But there is a body of common detours, less common and often less direct or less successful conversational paths and a long tail of edge cases or highly uncommon paths.

06:48

We cannot disregard or omit these as part of the agent design or implementation.

06:54

Think about all the things that can go wrong in a conversation, and all the unexpected or unsupported paths users might take.

07:01

The peacock analogy translates into a very simple design principle.

07:06

Always design your agent to deterministically cover the most common paths, followed by 80% of users.

07:13

Let LLMs handle the remaining 20% of paths that include edge cases and common detours.

07:20

Generative fallback is an example of a feature that uses Google's latest generative Large Language Models to

07:25

generate virtual agent responses when end user input does not match an intent or parameter for form filling.

07:34

The feature can be configured with a text prompt that instructs the LLM how to respond during conversation fallout.

07:41

In this example, the primary goal of the customer is to book a diving trip.

07:45

However, they ask if the bot can also book a diving course.

07:50

The agent is capable of handling the conversation by letting the customer know that he can't help him with the booking without escalating to a live agent.

07:58

The bottom line is that Large Language Models are great for use cases when divergent thinking can add value to the conversation.

08:06

However, keep in mind the following considerations before starting your design.

08:10

Business critical industries like finance, healthcare, and retail are subject to heavy regulation when it comes to data privacy and fairness.

08:20

This doesn't offer much flexibility in conversation design.

08:25

There are also potential security threats related to LLM based applications that process very sensitive user inputs, since they are subject to prompt injection.

08:35

And any non-deterministic response configured in the VA requires close scrutiny.

08:39

So strict quality assurance protocols are required when testing designs for non-deterministic responses.

00:00

## **Design Strategy**

Next, let's explore the key tenets of the most effective humanlike conversations.

00:05

Before diving into the topics that you'll cover, please note that Conversational Agents are also referred to as virtual agents.

00:13

In this section, you'll learn what conversation design strategy is and explore best practices.

00:18

We'll then discuss the steps in the conversation design process.

00:22

Conversation design is a discipline that draws from various design fields to create intuitive and natural conversational interfaces.

00:30

Like an architect, a conversation designer maps out user interactions by considering both user needs and technical limitations.

00:39

In order for the conversation designer to create a conversation in the most efficient way possible, they should try to anticipate the needs of their users as much as possible.

00:48

This often requires close collaboration with business stakeholders and developers to fine tune the conversational flows.

00:56

Some best practices to follow when designing a conversation include, but are not limited to the following: maximize usability, convert

01:04

pattern matching and decision trees to smart conversational design, and support varied experiences on multiple platforms, devices, languages, and dialects.

01:16

Your end goal when designing a conversation is to get the right information to the right person, in the right way, at the right time.

01:23

The conversation design process can be split in three main phases.

01:27

The first phase is to gather the design requirements.

01:31

Start by defining your users.

01:32

Who are they?

01:34

What are their goals?

01:34

What's their context?

01:38

You should also consider the technical limitations for the virtual agent.

01:42

This includes the level of effort the development will require and if the specified timeline will accommodate it.

01:49

Next, consider what use cases the virtual agent will support.

01:54

And lastly, focus on the other half of the conversation.

01:58

Who will be the voice of your actions?

02:00

This is when you get to create a persona that represents your brand and mission.

02:04

We'll explore this in the next section.

02:08

When defining your users, it's not just about their characteristics.

02:12

You also need to consider their actions.

02:14

For example, how would you describe their relevant moments in the journey?

02:18

What are their current ways of completing tasks?

02:21

The key is to accommodate as many users as possible, including those with diverse abilities.

02:26

By leveraging inclusive design strategies.

02:30

When exploring the technical capabilities and limitations, there are two areas of concern.

02:35

The first is the system.

02:37

It's important to identify what is and isn't possible.

02:40

You need to explore the capabilities of systems like Conversational Agents.

02:45

Consider user identification, data synchronization and handling overlapping sessions.

02:51

You also need to consider data.

02:52

When thinking about data, evaluate the format and quality of data you'll be using.

02:58

Assess the availability and length of session information.

03:02

Consider if content needs to be reformatted for text to speech or TTS.

03:07

While identifying key use cases, consider feasibility and impact.

03:12

Prioritize use cases that are feasible given available time and resources.

03:17

Also consider the user impact.

03:20

Prioritize use cases that will benefit a large number of users or that are highly visible.

03:25

For example, those that address common user needs or align with key business objectives.

03:31

And lastly, consider the user needs.

03:34

Conduct user research to understand the goals and language of the target user group.

03:39

This will help ensure that the virtual agent addresses the most pressing user needs, and provides a natural and intuitive user experience.

03:48

Next, start working on high level designs.

03:52

If you're new to conversation design, then start by learning about the conversation experience.

03:58

Then move on to writing sample dialogs and diagraming high level flows.

04:03

Focus on the spoken conversation first, as if it occurred face-to-face.

04:07

Design the screen interaction later, if you start designing with a screen in mind, it's easy to lose the thread of the conversation.

04:16

This might cause you to end up with a graphical interface that's not suitable for the conversation.

04:22

And the last phase of the process, you can specify the detailed design.

04:27

This involves testing and iterating on your designs.

04:30

Make sure your feature adequately covers the long tail of ways a conversation can deviate from the most common paths.

04:37

To account for this, you need to design for error handling and other unlikely or uncommon scenarios.

04:44

Once this is completed, you can scale your design.

04:48

You also need to consider what common assets can help you throughout this conversation design process.

04:53

First are discovery documents such as a future state definition and the business requirement document.

04:59

Among other things, these documents contain explanations of how interactions are currently handled in person.

05:06

This includes chat, voice, live agent, apps, legacy systems, and so on.

05:12

These documents describe the end goals for the engagement, including how conversations should be designed.

05:18

Refer to the additional resources to find out more about these documents.

05:23

Another key asset is the persona design sheet, which contains the overall description of the virtual agent's persona.

05:31

It's developed in collaboration with the customer care business.

05:34

It should be used as reference during and especially at the end of the conversation design process.

05:40

This makes sure the VA will sound and convey the personality traits and branding specified by the customer.

05:47

Again, refer to the additional resources for more information on the persona.

05:52

Next are conversation scripts.

05:54

These are scripts for each use case's conversations between the virtual agent and the customer.

06:00

They only contain the language and have no other information such as API information.

06:05

They're meant to be used when designing the experience and evaluating its quality.

06:10

For more information about these assets, consult the Additional Resources document.

06:15

And there's also the conversational design diagram.

06:19

This is a flowchart or a set of instructions contained in a visual representation.

06:24

These types of documents focus more on the solutions architecture.

06:28

It contains a description of what the agent says, not the actual verbiage.

06:33

It shows where the APIs are called and the multiple paths an end user can follow in each conversation.

06:39

Another asset is the Intent Taxonomy document.

06:43

This is a structured classification of user intents.

06:46

It should include intent names, utterance examples, and descriptions of the intents.

06:50

While intent design and development is part of the NLU design workstream, this document can be consulted when designing conversations.

07:01

And lastly, there's the entity or parameter interface specifications.

07:06

This document stipulates the required parameter formatting at the conversational presentation layer for chat and voice, names, and detailed API requirements.

07:16

Interface specifications need to include the name of the parameter, the type, and description.

07:22

Ideally, it also includes JSON payload examples.

07:26

Make sure to analyze API availability, including what can currently be used or what would need to be built or modified for conversational modality.

07:36

This includes user friendly names presented to the customer.

07:40

Webhooks and APIs will be covered in detail later on in the course.

00:00

## **Persona Design**

Let's now explore what is a persona in the context of conversation design and how to best create one.

00:06

Before diving into the lesson objectives, please note that Conversational Agents are also referred to as virtual agents.

00:13

In this section we'll explain what a persona is, elaborate on the link between brand characteristics and personas, discuss a persona's

00:22

personal traits, discuss conversational components, and close out with a checklist of guiding principles to keep in mind when creating a persona.

00:32

A persona can be understood broadly as a character either presented to or perceived by others, and typically inferred from the application's language choices.

00:42

When well crafted, a virtual agent persona creates the sense an end user is interacting with an individual, not a bot.

00:50

When creating a persona, you need to consider how you want to position the virtual agent.

00:55

Do you want to add more emotional sentiment to it?

00:58

What can you do to make sure that the persona has a more unified register across all of your agents?

01:05

Tone is important, and tone development and expression is a combination of design craft, technological capability, and both concrete and abstract aspects.

01:16

Identity, word choices, expressivity and virtual design all influence how users perceive and relate to a persona.

01:25

That's why it's important to know that not making intentional decisions about these traits is not neutral.

01:31

It simply means that you are giving up the potential influence over how the user perceives your agent persona.

01:38

Users are going to have judgments about its character no matter what you do, so

01:43

you might as well do everything you can to ensure their experience aligns with your intention.

01:48

Defining a clear system persona is vital to ensuring a consistent user experience that builds user trust.

01:55

Your persona can help provide users with a mental model for what the agent can and cannot do.

02:01

For example, consider the persona behind a chat bot that assists donors with blood donation eligibility questions.

02:08

It could be modeled after an idealized, helpful assistant, empathetic and trustworthy about donors' needs, and sensitive personal information.

02:17

A useful persona is at the intersection of three areas of influence.

02:21

It extends the brand message, fulfills a business objective, and connects to the target audience with defined characteristics.

02:30

The virtual agent persona should align with your existing brand characteristics to avoid creating a confusing image of your brand.

02:38

For example, Google brand characteristics can be described as useful, honest, engaging, and empathetic.

02:47

If you don't clearly define your brand persona, then your users will.

02:51

The gender and age, or age range of the virtual agent, informs the written communication style for chat applications.

02:59

These traits also inform the voice and text to speech interaction for voice applications.

03:05

For example, a sample profile can be non-binary and 25 to 35 years old in age.

03:12

The virtual agent's key character traits describe the virtual agent's personality, but personal traits are not limited to gender, age, and key character traits.

03:22

You can also consider creating a name, image, educational background, and so on for a richer profile.

03:29

Let's explore an example of how to specify the desired character traits of a virtual agent and the thought behind them.

03:36

Some of the most desirable character traits include approachable, happy and friendly, always available to greet the customer with a smile, smart and

03:45

simple, always available, trustworthy, which instills confidence, and most importantly, consistently reliable regardless of the time, day of the interaction and the channel.

03:58

That's what builds the customer trust.

04:01

Here are some examples of the rationale behind these traits.

04:04

For example, as we stated earlier, approachability suggests you're here to help.

04:09

While trustworthiness instills confidence.

04:12

A virtual agent's personal traits can be emphasized by the opening style.

04:17

For example, a formal introduction could be: "Good afternoon, customer name. How may I be of assistance today?"

04:24

Whereas a more informal virtual agent might open with: "Hello, how can I help?"

04:29

The use of "we" in the introduction can brand this virtual agent as a gender non-binary,

04:33

and it can position this agent as a bot of the company rather than a person.

04:40

Please note that the use of pronouns should be consistent across the entire virtual agent.

04:45

Here are some examples of these opening styles.

04:49

The way the virtual agent handles errors, like no input or no matches, is also an opportunity to portray its personality.

04:55

Discourse markers like "hmm", help create a more humanlike feeling.

05:01

Take note of these examples of general error handling prompts.

05:05

As a general rule, avoid too many instances of the word "sorry" in error handling.

05:09

During error handling, especially in the chat channel, what matters most are the instructions and explanations.

05:16

These are key to an agent resolving customer questions and problems.

05:21

Next is small talk.

05:23

This refers to an agent's handling of non use-case specific customer input.

05:27

Small talk can be important as well in portraying the character of your virtual agent.

05:32

"Acknowledgment" and "thanks" are the most common types of small talk in most virtual agents.

05:38

Small talk should be kept brief and generally only in response to a customer initiating it.

05:44

It should be leveraged only to reduce the friction for the customer during the interaction with a virtual agent.

05:49

When working towards a resolution of their contact reason.

05:53

The chat channel can also benefit from emojis when handling small talk.

05:58

If in line with a brand defined for the virtual agent persona.

06:01

The last conversation component is that of closing.

06:05

Closing completes an interaction between the customer and the virtual agent, and the event that the

06:09

virtual agent is unable to fully assist the customer, it hands off to a live agent.

06:15

When the customer reaches the end of the conversation flow.

06:18

The virtual agent can sign off with a closing statement.

06:21

However, consider how the interaction ends so that you can make this feel seamless for the end user.

06:28

These examples show different styles of closing, like: "Let me transfer you to a live agent to further assist you."

06:35

In the case of an agent handoff or goodbye, you can use: "Thank you for chatting", to end a session.

06:41

Alongside brand requirements and established business rules, there are a few additional guiding principles you should keep in mind when designing a persona.

06:50

First of all, don't allow the persona to take center stage.

06:54

Focus on the customer instead.

06:57

For example, instead of saying: "I made your reservation at Cascal", you can say: "Your reservation

07:02

at Cascal is all set." to keep the customer always at the center of the conversation.

07:08

The persona must also communicate with the appropriate level of confidence.

07:13

Instead of saying: "I didn't quite understand", state: "I didn't understand", it conveys more assertiveness.

07:21

The persona knows the customer's time is valuable.

07:24

So instead of providing long and convoluted answers, it should aim for brevity to expedite the resolution of the customer's query.

07:32

Look at the differences between these two examples.

07:34

In the first example, the virtual agent provides detailed information about each available flight.

07:41

In the second example, they only provide the essential information like: "I'm showing four flights, here they are."

07:48

In the second example, the agent is able to resolve the customer's query much faster.

07:53

The virtual agent persona should also minimize social distance.

07:57

Instead of saying: "Please say the reason why you're calling?", the agent could ask: "What are you calling about?"

08:03

This is much more immediate.

08:04

Avoid "Please" as much as possible when making requests.

08:08

"Please" in directives communicate social distance as opposed to social intimacy and solidarity.

08:14

Only use it when the user is being significantly inconvenienced.

08:18

You should also make sure that the virtual agent persona talks like everyday people.

08:22

This is to avoid as much technical jargon and legalese as possible.

08:27

The persona knows the customer's time is valuable.

08:29

So instead of providing long and convoluted answers, the virtual agent should aim for brevity to expedite the resolution of the customer's query.

08:38

Customers don't know what intents are, so having the agent mentioned that they found something that match their intent is irrelevant and unnecessary.

08:46

It's quicker and more natural to reply with a simple: "Sure, here you are", then close the interaction.

08:52

You must also make sure that the virtual agent persona never sounds condescending.

08:57

Avoid terms like "well", which typically introduces a response that is inferior in quality or quantity to what the listener expects or deserves.

09:06

In the following example, the assistant uses "well" and it comes off as condescending.

09:11

It's probably best to avoid it altogether.

09:14

Implicit in all of this is the fact that the persona should be helpful and proactive.

09:20

So instead of rejecting the user's request without alternative, it should proactively attempt to provide a path to resolve the customer query.

09:28

In this example, instead of saying" "Unfortunately, I don't see any drivers within 20 minutes of you, but you can

09:33

try again another time", the virtual agent could say: "Would you like me to check again in 2 or 3 minutes?"

09:41

And lastly, the virtual agent persona should avoid drawing unnecessary attention to problems and or errors. So instead of saying: "Sorry,

09:48

this must be really frustrating for you, try saying the date one more time", You could instead say: "Still didn't get that?

09:56

Try saying the date one more time.

09:58

" For instance, June 30th.

Autoplay

### Transcript

00:00

## **Writing Conversation Scripts**

Next, let's delve into the writing process for the creation of a good conversation script.

00:05

But first, please note that Conversational Agents are also referred to as virtual agents.

00:11

The goal of this section is to write a conversation script that serves as the basis to configure a chat steering bot that only does head intent detection or HID.

00:21

In this section, you'll learn about the key steps of the writing process, how to create a sample template, and how to craft a powerful welcome message.

00:31

You'll then focus on the welcome message pre-expansions and disambiguation.

00:37

Remember, the persona design drives how a customer experiences the conversation.

00:42

A persona guides the designer throughout the process to carefully choose which specific words they'll use in each prompt.

00:49

The recommended approach to writing humanlike scripts is like constructing a house.

00:54

You begin with the foundation, then add the plumbing, followed by the walls, and lastly, select the color of the paint.

01:01

In essence, you begin with the big picture in mind to lay the framework for the conversation.

01:07

You then address the necessary aspects to resolve the issue at hand.

01:11

Writing scripts involves numerous iterations to capture increasing detail, add that human touch, and create the desired persona.

01:19

Just like with a house where you start from the foundation, when constructing a conversation it's a best practice to start with a big picture and then add the details.

01:28

The conversations structure should be your starting point.

01:31

Once you have a template, enhance it to give more structure to the use case.

01:36

Then enhance the design with side questions and additional intents to redirect the conversation towards the solution while addressing customers concerns.

01:45

And finally, detail the persona by carefully choosing which specific words to use in each prompt to better drive how a customer experiences the conversation.

01:55

Following the house analogy, let's explore the process to write conversation scripts.

02:00

The first step is to create a foundation for the conversation script.

02:04

This is the template or general structure of the expected conversation.

02:09

Next, you need to enhance the template to give more structure to each use case.

02:14

But conversations can go off script.

02:17

That's why we need action reinforcements.

02:20

Do this by enhancing the design with side questions and additional intents.

02:24

This helps to redirect the conversation towards the solution while addressing customer's unexpected concerns.

02:32

Finally, add all the decorations and details to the conversation.

02:36

Throughout this module, we'll learn all these different elements of conversations and will build a conversation script as we go.

02:43

Let's explore each step in more detail, starting with the foundation.

02:47

Conversations in customer service follow a very specific and predefined structure to which humans naturally adhere.

02:54

Following this basic template is so seamless and natural that humans typically don't realize they are doing it.

03:00

When designing conversations for customer service settings, it's critical to be aware of this template.

03:06

This is so the overall experience remains human like, regardless of use case or even industry

03:12

Conversations in customer service are organized into three big phases: opening sequence, main sequence, and closing sequence.

03:21

These sequences start and end at very specific points and with very specific actions.

03:26

You should try to adhere to these phases when you write the overall template, so that you craft a human like experience for the end user.

03:34

Let's explore an example of a customer service conversation between two real people to identify the three phases.

03:41

First is the welcome message or opening sequence.

03:44

It's short and simple, regardless of persona and brand.

03:48

This opening sequence takes less than five seconds in length, and it ends with an offer to serve, which encourages the customer to describe why they're calling.

03:58

Then the conversation proceeds to the service request.

04:01

Here, the customer makes their request and describes why they're calling.

04:05

This is the head intent or HI in agent design terminology.

04:10

This is followed by an interrogative series.

04:13

In this phase, the agent and customer engage in a series of questions that helps the agent gather more information from the customer to deliver the appropriate solution.

04:22

Typically, in a simple FAQ, this phase is skipped.

04:26

Next is the service response.

04:29

Once the agent has gathered all the needed information, they provide the best possible solution.

04:34

This is the fulfillment for the head intent.

04:37

What follows is the acceptance action.

04:39

This essential step shouldn't be overlooked.

04:42

In unscripted human customer service conversations, agents build their turns and interactions to get to this precise point.

04:50

This phase confirms that they provided the best solution to the customer.

04:55

In other words, it's the culmination of a job well done.

04:58

So it must be included in your conversation template.

05:02

And finally, the conversation ends with a closing sequence.

05:06

Typically, humans in customer service move on to simply thanking each other and saying goodbye.

05:11

Depending on the industry and the variety of products a business might offer, agents may

05:15

optionally check with the customer if they have any other issue that needs to be addressed.

05:21

Now that we know the overall structure of a conversation in customer service, we'll start by laying out the basic structure which will expand and enrich in later steps.

05:31

For now, we can skip the interrogative series and focus only on the service request, the service response and the acceptance.

05:39

Then replace these three actions with words.

05:41

For example, a conversation to book a flight could go as shown.

05:45

Make sure the virtual agent's response matches the customer's intent.

05:49

And don't forget the acceptance.

05:51

This will be your basic template.

05:54

In the above template, the representative replies with information that the customer didn't provide, such as the departure date.

06:00

This is because this information is collected in the interrogative series, also known as happy path.

06:06

In the next steps, you'll fill these missing turns.

06:09

Also, since this is a steering agent, the conversational agent won't be able to provide a service

06:14

response and will transfer the user out of Conversational Agents as soon as it collects a head intent.

06:21

But it's important for now to have the most important conversational pieces in place so we

06:25

can build the rest around it and have an overall view of how the conversation will unfold.

06:33

Let's recap.

06:34

The opening sequence includes a short welcome message and ends with an offer to serve, which encourages the customer to describe the request.

06:42

The welcome message is generally performed by the agent in one single turn.

06:46

In naturally occurring conversations, the agent delivers at most the following three actions in the following order.

06:53

It starts with the agent answering the call with something like: "Hello, welcome to company A", or, "Thanks for contacting customer service."

07:01

Then the agent provides a company and or personal identification.

07:05

Like: "This is Joe speaking from company A."

07:08

Lastly, there's an offer to serve such as: "How can I help you?", or: "What can I do for you today?"

07:14

Agents may not explicitly state all three actions, but imply them through context.

07:19

This example shows naturally occurring conversations between human agents and customers where the answer, identification, and offer to serve have been marked where appropriate.

07:29

For instance, the agent could say: "Jack Camera, this is Tara speaking, may I help you?"

07:36

This example does not have the answer action.

07:39

Or the agent could say: "Hello, how can I help you?", which lacks the identification action.

07:44

Now that you have the basic template and know how the welcome message in a conversation in customer service works, you can add the skeleton for the welcome message.

07:53

When writing the welcome message, make sure it only has at most an answer, an identification, and an offer to serve.

08:01

When writing the message, keep it simple and short.

08:04

For the identification action, you may want to have your VA self-identify as a virtual agent for compliance reasons, but check with your customers.

08:13

For instance, in this example, the virtual agent will perform the three main actions with the verbiage, "Hello, this is Trip World's virtual assistant, how can I help you?"

08:24

Next is the main sequence.

08:26

This will become more relevant in future modules, but for now, it's important to have

08:29

a general idea of how the main sequence of a conversation and customer service looks like.

08:36

This starts with a service request, where the customer explains his or her query and the head intent is collected.

08:43
